title: QUIC Interop Runner Multiconnect Sequential Dispatch Architecture
purpose: Describe how the interop harness routes the child-process-only `multiconnect` testcase through the real endpoint-host transfer path one URL at a time without widening the supported protocol surface.
design_summary: The harness adds a small sequential transfer-plan builder for the `multiconnect` testcase. It parses every space-separated client `REQUESTS` URL, requires `https` and a shared host/port, resolves the same managed endpoint-host path used by transfer, and then opens one managed connection per URL in order. The client side creates a fresh managed connection per request, emits exactly one HTTP/0.9 GET on that connection, waits for response progress on that one connection, and fails the testcase honestly if response bytes or EOF stall instead of leaving the child process hung behind the outer runner timeout. The server side supports both the known-plan shape used by local paired smoke tests and the empty-`REQUESTS` shape used by the external runner. With a known plan, it accepts one connection per configured request. With an empty plan, it listens as an open sequential server, derives each mounted file from the observed HTTP/0.9 request target, sends the response after the request line is parsed, and starts a bounded post-response linger task so peer-close observation does not block later accepts or handshakes. The harness no longer initiates application close solely to end a completed response connection; the library close path still protects 1-RTT application CONNECTION_CLOSE frames if a local close is requested elsewhere. The listener host also treats UDP socket connection-reset/refused indications as per-datagram noise so one short-lived peer cannot stop the shared server socket. Requirement-home tests prove the sequential happy path, open-plan server sequencing, the request/response markers, the stalled-response failure path, and malformed-input rejection without claiming parallel connection handling, migration, retry enablement, `0-RTT`, key update, or public API widening.
alternatives_considered: Keep returning `127` for `multiconnect` and wait for a broader endpoint-host redesign. Rejected because the testcase can be routed honestly today using the existing active-phase transfer path.
Build a generalized multipath or parallel-connection dispatcher. Rejected because that would widen the supported surface beyond the single bounded testcase slice requested here.
edge_cases_and_constraints: All `REQUESTS` entries must share the same host and port so the sequential plan stays on the same endpoint-host path.

Malformed client `REQUESTS` input must fail before any transport success is claimed, while an empty server `REQUESTS` list is valid only for supported server-role dispatch paths that can derive the requested path from inbound HTTP/0.9 traffic.

The server must serve after the HTTP/0.9 request line because waiting for request EOF can deadlock the bidirectional response stream against peer implementations that keep the write side open until the response completes.
Post-response peer-close linger must not block the open-plan accept loop; otherwise one completed connection can prevent a later sequential connection from completing its handshake under loss.
A completed response must not be ended by a server-initiated application close, because peers can classify that as a remote application error even when the body bytes arrived.
A response that stalls after the client has sent the HTTP/0.9 request line must fail the testcase and remove any partial output instead of waiting indefinitely for the external runner timeout.
The implementation must not claim migration, parallel connection handling, or any public API widening.
data_and_state_considerations: Sequential plans are derived from the immutable runner request list. qlog capture remains a per-run disposable scope, and the sequential testcase uses the same mounted `/www` and `/downloads` paths as the existing transfer slice without introducing new durable state.
risks: A reused connection-options object could hide a host/port mismatch if the plan builder does not enforce consistency or if the client reuses the wrong remote endpoint across sequential connects.

The sequential server loop must remain bounded to one accepted connection and one served request per URL or the testcase could accidentally drift into a transfer-style multi-request connection or a parallel-connection claim.
Malformed input should fail before listener startup so the negative path stays honest.
If the client waits forever on a stalled response stream, the testcase can mask the real transport outcome behind a runner-level timeout and leave misleading partial downloads behind.
If post-response linger stays inline in the open-plan server loop, the live runner can time out a later sequential connection even though the previous response completed.
If the listener treats per-peer UDP reset/refused signals as fatal to the shared socket, one closed connection can prevent unrelated sequential accepts from making progress.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This design covers only the sequential `multiconnect` dispatch slice. The broader runner dispatch plan and remaining unsupported testcase behavior stay under `REQ-QUIC-INT-0012`, `REQ-QUIC-INT-0013`, and `REQ-QUIC-INT-0014`.
